A.translation: A huge ship was near the gate.
It is necessary to depart immediately.
notes: 'necesse est + inf' — 'it is necessary to X'
accept 'one must leave immediately' / 'leaving at once is necessary'

A.translation: The son was braver than the father.
That goddess was the most beautiful of all.

A.latin: tam tristis erat uxor quam maritus.
A.translation: The wife was as sad as the husband.
notes: accept 'the wife was just as sad as the husband'

A.translation: This journey will be very easy.

A.latin: multi comites iam fuerunt, sed nunc pauci sunt.
A.translation: Many companions have now existed, but now there are few.
notes: 'fuerunt' perfect = have been / existed / were there
accept 'there have been many companions, but now there are few' / 'many companions existed before, but now few remain'

A.latin: solus iuvenis in silva erat, perterritus et iratus.
A.translation: The young man was alone in the wood, terrified and angry.
notes: 'solus' = alone / only — accept 'the lone young man was in the wood' / 'the young man was alone in the wood'

A.latin: unus miles fortissimus erat, sed ceteri fortes non erant.
A.translation: One soldier was very brave, but the rest were not brave.

A.latin: tantus et dirus erat deus; omnes igitur perterriti fuerunt.
A.translation: So great and dreadful was the god; therefore all were terrified.

B.translation: The leader is not able to order the soldiers.
notes: 'iubeo + acc' (here no 'acc+inf' construction — accept 'order the soldiers' as generic command)
'nolite + inf' = negative plural imperative — 'don't X (you all)'
accept 'Do not escape!' / 'Don't run away!' / 'Don't flee!'

B.translation: It is necessary for the father to free his son.
notes: 'necesse est + dat + inf' — 'it is necessary for X to Y'
accept 'the father must free his son'

B.latin: comes reginam conspicere volebat, sed non potuit.
B.translation: The companion wanted to catch sight of the queen, but could not.
Many soldiers began to attack the city.
"Don't leave me behind, son!" said the father.

B.latin: possum te audire, sed non possum te videre.
B.translation: I am able to hear you, but I am not able to see you.
notes: accept 'I can hear you, but I cannot see you'

B.latin: illa dea voluit ad insulam navigare.
B.translation: That goddess wanted to sail to the island.

B.latin: puella tristis coepit clamare, quod pater advenire non potuit.
B.translation: The sad girl began to shout, because the father was not able to arrive.
notes: accept 'could not come / reach them' for 'advenire non potuit'

B.latin: uxor maritum videre volebat, sed maritus iam discesserat.
B.translation: The wife wanted to see her husband, but the husband had already departed.
notes: accept 'had already left' / 'had already gone away' for 'iam discesserat'

C.translation: The son goes to the wood.

C.latin: milites ad urbem ibant, sed mox redierunt.
C.translation: The soldiers were going to the city, but soon returned.
notes: 'ibant' imperfect of 'eo' — 'were going' / 'used to go' / 'kept going'

C.latin: hic maritus per silvam iit, deinde prope insulam advenit.
C.translation: This husband went through the wood, then arrived near the island.
The companion returned with many gifts.

C.latin: dux ex urbe iit, et in silvam redire constituit.
C.translation: The leader went out of the city, and decided to return into the wood.
notes: accept 'go back to the wood' for 'in silvam redire'

C.latin: filius ad portam ibit, et pater domum redibit.
C.translation: The son will go to the gate, and the father will return home.
notes: accept 'will come back' / 'will go back' for 'redibit'

C.latin: simulac uxor domum rediit, cibum accepit.
C.translation: As soon as the wife returned home, she received food.

C.latin: iuvenis per urbem iter fecit; tandem domum rediit.
C.translation: The young man made a journey through the city; finally he returned home.
notes: accept 'travelled through the city' / 'went on a journey through the city'

C.latin: dea a deis rediit, et statim domum iit.
C.translation: The goddess returned from the gods, and immediately went home.
notes: 'a/ab + abl' with persons = from (also can be agent 'by' — here separation 'from')
accept 'came back from the gods' / 'went back from the gods'

C.latin: dux in urbe erat, sed cum comitibus in silvam iit; mox redire constituit.
C.translation: The leader was in the city, but with his companions went into the wood; he soon decided to return.
